title: Tanker Report - Week 38
summary:
VLCCs
It has been another positive week for VLCC owners. A heavy programme from Basrah for the first decade in October, saw a surge of enquiry from charterers, and combined with delays in both China and India, the tonnage list thinned somewhat. In the Middle East Gulf rates for 270,000 tonnes going long east have gained over 13 points with WS 63 having been agreed and rumours circulating of WS 65 now on subjects.

children.title: VLCCs
children.summary: It has been another positive week for VLCC owners. A heavy programme from Basrah for the first decade in October, saw a surge of enquiry from charterers, and combined with delays in both China and India, the tonnage list thinned somewhat.

children.title: Suezmaxes
children.summary: Fuel oil traders showed more interest off the Continent for trips going east and this combined with a firmer VLCC market and a significant number of outstanding cargoes in West Africa, saw rates finally firm from a settled WS 57.5 up to WS 65 paid for Triathlon by Total. Black Sea has been steady with last seen here being Yasa Southern Cross to Chevron at WS 60.

children.title: Aframaxes
children.summary: The Mediterranean has seen a very active week but with the market here swamped with tonnage, rates have remained under pressure and have even softened marginally from WS 75 down to WS 70/72.5 level. The September program in the Baltic is all but covered now and there were reports of an outstanding cargo there receiving seven offers.

children.title: Panamaxes
children.summary: With a continued weak panamax market in the Caribbean which presently sits in the high WS 70s, there is no shortage of ballasters to the Continent. This is keeping a lid on rates here, where the market remains around WS 87.5 for 55,000 tonnes from ARA to US Gulf.

children.title: Clean
children.summary: The market for 37,000 tonnes from Continent to USAC has suffered as a lack of enquiry combined with plentiful tonnage saw rates ease from WS 110 at the start of the week down to barely WS 100. In contrast the backhaul trade for 38,000 tonnes has been extremely active as the diesel arb to the Continent opened up - subsequently rates have soared from WS 95 a week ago to around WS 150.
